summary:
Kabuki Theater (c. 1603–present) was one of the most important artistic innovations of the [[event:2376|Edo period]] — a dramatic form that combined elaborate costumes, stylized acting, dance, and music into a spectacular entertainment primarily for the merchant and artisan classes (chōnin) who were excluded from the aristocratic Nō theater. Kabuki is traditionally said to have been founded by Izumo no Okuni, a female shrine attendant who performed suggestive dance dramas in Kyoto around 1603; the form quickly became popular but also controversial for its association with prostitution. The Tokugawa government banned women from performing in 1629, leading to all-male kabuki, with onnagata (male actors specializing in female roles) becoming a central artistic tradition.

Kabuki flourished across the Edo period, developing a rich repertoire of plays — historical dramas (jidaimono) dealing with samurai conflicts, and domestic dramas (sewamono) depicting the lives of townspeople, including tragic love stories often involving conflicts between duty (giri) and human feeling (ninjō). Playwrights like Chikamatsu Monzaemon (who also wrote for puppet theater) created enduring works that are still performed today. The Edo period also saw the development of distinctive performance styles — mie poses (dramatic frozen moments), kumadori face paint, and hanamichi (the runway extending through the audience) — that gave kabuki its unmistakable visual character. Kabuki was designated an Intangible Cultural Heritage by UNESCO in 2005, representing one of Japan's most distinctive and enduring theatrical traditions.
